request.question: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?

title: Rick and Morty
HarmonQuest
The Sarah Silverman Program
Harmontown
Great Minds with Dan Harmon
Acceptable.TV
Basic Rocket Science
Community (TV series)

Introduction to Finality
sentences: Rick and Morty is an American adult animated science-fiction sitcom created by Justin Roiland and Dan Harmon for Cartoon Network's late-night programming block Adult Swim.

The series follows the misadventures of cynical mad scientist Rick Sanchez and his fretful, easily influenced grandson Morty Smith, who split their time between domestic family life and interdimensional adventures.
Roiland voices the series' eponymous characters, with the voice talent of Chris Parnell, Spencer Grammer, and Sarah Chalke providing the rest of the family.
It premiered on December 2, 2013.
HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.
The show is part animated, part live action.
Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.
Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.

The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.

The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.
Harmontown is a weekly live comedy podcast that began airing on June 6, 2012.
It is hosted by writer Dan Harmon, best known as the creator of the sitcom "Community", and actor Jeff B. Davis, best known for his work on the improvisational comedy series "Whose Line Is It Anyway?
" "Harmontown" began as a monthly live comedy show in Los Angeles, California at the NerdMelt Showroom on May 23, 2011, but the show became weekly after Harmon was fired from "Community".
Since the summer of 2012, each episode of "Harmontown" has been recorded and released as a podcast by Feral Audio.
Great Minds with Dan Harmon is an American comedy television series that premiered on February 25, 2016 on the History channel.

The series stars writer Dan Harmon (creator of "Community" and co-creator of "Rick and Morty") and his assistant Spencer Crittenden as themselves, who transport a series of historical figures to the present.
The series is part of History's "Night Class" programming block and was available to stream on History's website and YouTube channel.
The videos have since been removed but short clips are still available on YouTube.
Acceptable.TV is a television program created by Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab that aired on VH1 from March 23 to May 11, 2007.
Each show was composed of several mini-episodes created by the Acceptable.TV staff, with one mini-episode that was submitted by a viewer.
After each episode viewers would be able to vote online for their two favorites.
The two that received the most votes will be continued in the following episode, and the remaining three would be cancelled and replaced by new mini-shows.
The show was adapted from Harmon and Schrab's Channel 101 screenings, and the cast was composed of various Channel 101 contributors.
"Basic Rocket Science" is the fourth episode of the second season of "Community".
It was originally broadcast on October 14, 2010, on NBC.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.

The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.
It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.
The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.
The show stars:
"Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.
It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.
This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

hop1_docs: List of Community characters | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.  The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.  The show stars:

Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television | "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television" is the series finale of the sitcom "Community", serving as the thirteenth episode of its sixth season.  It was written by series creator Dan Harmon and Chris McKenna, and directed by Rob Schrab.  It is the 110th episode overall and was first released on Yahoo! Screen in the United States on June 2, 2015.

Dan Harmon | Daniel Harmon (born January 3, 1973) is an American writer, producer, and voice actor.  Harmon created and produced the NBC comedy television series "Community", co-created the Adult Swim animated series "Rick and Morty", and co-founded the alternative television network/website Channel 101.  Harmon published "You'll Be Perfect When You're Dead" in 2013.  He also hosts a weekly eponymous podcast, "Harmontown."

Community (TV series) | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.  The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.  It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.

Introduction to Finality | "Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.  It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.  This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

The Sarah Silverman Program | The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.

HarmonQuest | HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.  The show is part animated, part live action.  Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.  Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.  The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.
response.summary_1:
- Fact: [4] "Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon." (quote: "Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon")
- Fact: [1] Passage confirms same: show set at "Greendale Community College" (paraphrase).
- Fact: [2] Passage shows episode-level data exist on other pages (paraphrase: series finale titled "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television", "110th episode overall").
- Fact: [5] Passage gives another episode title ("Introduction to Finality") showing episode articles exist but does not provide episode 4.
- Bridge entity (missing): the specific episode title for "the fourth episode" of Community is not present in the supplied passages.
- Ambiguity to resolve: whether "fourth episode" means (A) the fourth aired episode overall, (B) Season 1 episode 4 (air order), or (C) fourth produced (production order). Do not assume which is intended.
- Priority next-hop retrievals:
  1. Fetch "List of Community episodes" (Wikipedia page or equivalent). Extract: the table row for overall episode number 4 (Episode no. = 4) — title, season, season_episode number, air date, and production code.
  2. Fetch "Community (season 1) — Episodes" section. Extract: Season 1, Episode 4 title and airdate (confirm airing order and title).
  3. Fetch "Community (TV series)" page — "Episodes" or "Episode list" section. Extract: any episode list or link to the episode list page.
  4. If production order vs airing order remains ambiguous, extract the "Production code" column from the List of episodes page for the relevant early episodes (episodes 1–6) to determine produced vs aired order.

Do not provide a final episode title until one of the above pages confirms the exact episode by the requested ordering.
response.hop2_query: Community episode 4 title List of Community episodes
hop2_docs: List of Kaizoku Sentai Gokaiger episodes | This is a list of episodes for Kaizoku Sentai Gokaiger, the 35th installment of the long running Japanese Super Sentai franchise.  As a special anniversary edition, several episodes of Kaizoku Sentai Gokaiger pay a tribute to previous Super Sentai series, titled similarly to said series' episode titles.  Examples include episode 5 which follows "Tokusou Sentai Dekaranger"' s format of using an English title written in katakana, episodes 11 and 12 which match "Samurai Sentai Shinkenger"' s kanji-only titles, and episodes 18 and 29 which follow "Bakuryū Sentai Abaranger"' s gimmick of including the word abare (アバレ) in the episode title.

List of Kamen Rider Den-O episodes | This is a list of episodes of the 2007 Japanese television series Kamen Rider Den-O.  Episode titles prefaced with a red line signify an episode centered on the story of Kamen Rider Den-O.  Episode titles prefaced with a green line signify an episode centered on the story of Kamen Rider Zeronos.

List of Glenn Martin, DDS episodes | This is a list of episodes for the TV series "Glenn Martin, DDS".  Some episodes were titled differently in the U.K. Where this is the case the U.K. title is listed underneath the U.S. title.  Some of these alternative titles have also been used in the U.S. for repeat showings.

List of The Scooby-Doo Show episodes | This is a list of episodes from "The Scooby-Doo Show".  The episode titles given for the show reflect Hanna-Barbera studio records, as no on-screen titles were given for most episodes.
List of This Is Daniel Cook episodes | The following is an episode list for the series "This is Daniel Cook".  All episode titles begin with the series title.

List of Kamen Rider Kiva episodes | This is a list of episodes of the 2008 Japanese television series Kamen Rider Kiva.  Each episode title combines a word or phrase relating to music and a phrase more directly related to the episode.  A symbol from musical notation is used to separate these two halves of the episodes' titles (an exception is the finale, which uses the music end barline at the end of the title).

List of Hikaru no Go episodes | This is a list of episodes for the anime series "Hikaru no Go".  This lists every episode, starting with the English title as they aired on Toonami Jetstream, followed by the title that was used on the original Japanese episode on TV Tokyo.  The anime more or less follows the same storyline as the manga.  A short summary follows, introducing the episode and referring to some of the gags.  There are a total of 75 episodes, excluding a 2004 special showing what happens after episode 75.  The final three episodes were released in English dub February 2011 when the series was put up for download on the iTunes store.
